title: MT Executor orchestrator integrating job queue + mailbox + loop control + backpressure
goal: Land the top-level `MicroTaskExecutor` orchestrator that ties X.1 mailbox primitives + X.2 job queue + loop control + cancellation + scheduler + outcome recording into a single executor loop. This is the entrypoint a worker session calls to execute MTs end-to-end with all preservation-slice invariants enforced.
expected_diff_shape: New Rust module src/backend/handshake_core/src/process_ledger/mt_executor.rs with `MicroTaskExecutor::run(executor_identity, config) -> impl Future`; integration tests under src/backend/handshake_core/tests/mt_executor_integration_tests.rs.
unblock_conditions: All X.1 and X.2 primitives merged so the executor orchestrator has every dependency available.
implementation_notes: Top-level loop: `loop { (1) check cancellation token; (2) call FairScheduler::next_for_executor (MT-187); (3) if Some(job) -> claim via MicroTaskQueue (MT-184); (4) acquire mailbox lease for job.mailbox_thread_id via LeaseManager (MT-180) with router permission check (MT-181); (5) load MT contract from job.mt_contract_path; (6) for each iteration up to MtLoopControlBudget (MT-185): (a) record_checkpoint; (b) check backpressure for outgoing mailbox messages (MT-182); (c) execute the MT body (delegates to a Coder trait — coder impl is out of scope here, MT executor receives a `MtCoderHandle: trait` provided by caller); (d) parse completion_signal; (e) if Verification needed -> post micro_task_verification_needed message to mailbox (MT-179) and await; (f) if iteration succeeds -> record outcome (MT-188) Success branch; (g) if iteration fails -> record outcome (MT-188) Failure branch which routes escalation; (h) check cancellation token between every iteration boundary (MT-186); }`. After completion, post `micro_task_completion_report` mailbox message (MT-179) and release lease (MT-180). Errors at any step: emit FR-EVT-MT-EXEC-ERROR with structured cause, record outcome=Failure, do NOT panic. The MtCoderHandle trait surface: `trait MtCoderHandle { async fn execute(&self, ctx: MtExecutionContext) -> Result<MtIterationResult, MtCoderError>; }` — keeps the coder impl pluggable so cluster C can wire LlamaCppRuntime/CandleRuntime adapters and tests can use a stub. Integration tests: (a) end-to-end Happy Path — stub coder returns Success on iteration 1, verify outcome + mailbox completion message + lease released; (b) Failure path — stub returns Failure twice, verify escalation tier moves T7B->T7BAlt, mailbox escalation message posted; (c) Cancellation path — request cancellation mid-iteration, verify Cancelled state + cleanup; (d) Backpressure path — flood mailbox to saturate, verify Deny propagated as a soft retry not a hard failure; (e) Lease lost mid-iteration (simulated expiry) — verify graceful re-acquire or Abandon outcome.
risk_if_missed: Without the orchestrator, all X.1/X.2 primitives are unwired libraries; no executor session can actually run an MT through the preservation-slice contracts; the cluster X fold delivers zero end-to-end behavior.
validator_focus: Orchestrator never panics — every error path records outcome=Failure with structured cause; cancellation observed within bounded latency; lease re-acquire path proven (does not silently double-claim).

minimum_controls: End-to-end test asserts zero panics across 5 happy/failure/cancellation paths.
Lease re-acquire after simulated expiry asserts router permission re-check (not stale token reuse).
MtCoderHandle trait is sealed or marker-trait-protected so unauthorized coders cannot be substituted at runtime.
